title_en: Introduction: The Two Jalals and the Genesis of Tafsir al-Jalalain
content_en:
Tafsir al-Jalalain — the Commentary of the Two Jalals — holds a unique place in the history of Quranic exegesis. It is the collaborative work of two scholars who share not only the name Jalal al-Din but also a shared vision: to produce a concise, accessible, and linguistically sound tafsir that students in traditional Islamic education could master without being overwhelmed. The result is one of the most widely read and memorized tafsirs in Islamic history, used from the madrasas of West Africa to the pesantrens of Southeast Asia.

The first author, Imam Jalal al-Din al-Mahalli (791–864 AH / 1389–1459 CE), was an Egyptian scholar of the Shafi'i school known for his mastery of jurisprudence, usul, and linguistics. He began the tafsir starting from Surah al-Kahf (18) through Surah al-Nas (114), and then wrote the commentary on Surah al-Fatiha. He did not live to complete the first half of the Quran.

It fell to his student, Imam Jalal al-Din al-Suyuti (849–911 AH / 1445–1505 CE), to complete the work. Al-Suyuti was one of the most prolific scholars in Islamic history, the author of hundreds of works spanning every discipline of the Islamic sciences. He revered his teacher deeply and, after al-Mahalli's death, undertook to complete the commentary on Surahs al-Baqarah through al-Isra (2–17) in a style that matched his teacher's approach so closely that the seam between them is nearly imperceptible.

Al-Suyuti reportedly completed his portion in approximately forty days — a feat that testifies both to his extraordinary command of the exegetical tradition and to the deliberate brevity that characterizes the work. He was careful not to introduce his own theological digressions or extend the commentary beyond what was necessary to illuminate the text.

The tafsir operates on a single, consistent principle: explain what is necessary and nothing more. It glosses individual words, resolves grammatical ambiguities, identifies the antecedents of pronouns, supplies the objects of verbs where Arabic ellipsis leaves them implied, and provides the basic historical context for verses with specific occasions of revelation. What it does not do is engage in extended theological debate, multiply narrations of asbab al-nuzul, or enter into jurisprudential disputes. For a student encountering the Quran's Arabic text, it functions as an indispensable guide to what the words mean before deeper study begins.

The canonical manuscript tradition places al-Suyuti's commentary on al-Baqarah through al-Isra at the beginning of the text, followed by al-Mahalli's commentary on al-Kahf through al-Nas, ending with al-Fatiha. This arrangement means the reader passes through both scholars' work as a single continuous commentary on the whole Quran.

Traditional madrasa students in many parts of the world study Tafsir al-Jalalain as the gateway to Quranic exegesis — after mastering Arabic grammar through works like the Ajurrumiyyah and studying introductory fiqh, they turn to al-Jalalain as their first full tafsir. The experience trains the student to read the Quran with grammatical precision and basic interpretive awareness before graduating to longer, more detailed commentaries like Tafsir Ibn Kathir or Tafsir al-Qurtubi.
